Multi-Omic Integration: TCR Repertoire × ELISpot

TCR Repertoire Analysis — Notebook 07

Joshua Luthy

2026-03-15

Analysis Overview

This notebook integrates TCR repertoire data with ELISpot functional immune response data to build a multi-omic picture of the relationship between clonal expansion and T cell effector function.

ELISpot (Enzyme-Linked Immunospot) measures cytokine secretion at the single-cell level, providing a functional readout of T cell reactivity against tumor-associated antigens. By correlating ELISpot responses with TCR clonal expansion metrics, we can assess whether the clonotypes expanding post-infusion are driving measurable anti-tumor immunity.

Assay panel:

  • IFN-γ — primary measure of Th1/CTL effector function
  • IL-2 — marker of T cell proliferative capacity and memory potential
  • Granzyme B — direct cytotoxic effector molecule

Target antigens: WT1, PRAME, Survivin, NY-ESO-1, MAGE-A3

# TCR data
auc_metrics    <- read_csv("../data/processed/clonal_auc_metrics.csv") %>%
  mutate(clinical_response = factor(clinical_response, levels = resp_levels))

diversity      <- read_csv("../data/processed/diversity_metrics.csv") %>%
  mutate(clinical_response = factor(clinical_response, levels = resp_levels))

longitudinal   <- read_csv("../data/processed/longitudinal_clonal_tracking.csv") %>%
  mutate(clinical_response = factor(clinical_response, levels = resp_levels))

# ELISpot data
elispot        <- read_csv("../data/processed/elispot_data.csv") %>%
  mutate(clinical_response = factor(clinical_response, levels = resp_levels),
         timepoint = factor(timepoint, levels = c("Pre", "Wk2", "Wk4", "Wk8", "Wk12")))

elispot_summary <- read_csv("../data/processed/elispot_summary.csv") %>%
  mutate(clinical_response = factor(clinical_response, levels = resp_levels))

patient_meta   <- read_csv("../data/raw/patient_metadata.csv")
3
Cytokines Measured
5
Target Antigens
14/30
Positive Responses
5
Timepoints

ELISpot Response Overview

IFN-γ Responses by Antigen and Patient

# Max IFN-g response per patient per antigen
heatmap_data <- elispot_summary %>%
  mutate(max_ifng_capped = pmin(max_ifng, 500))

ggplot(heatmap_data, aes(x = antigen, y = reorder(patient_id, -as.numeric(clinical_response)),
                          fill = max_ifng_capped)) +
  geom_tile(color = "#1e3348", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = max_ifng),
            color = ifelse(heatmap_data$max_ifng > 100, "#0a0f14", "#7b9db5"),
            size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#152231", mid = "#7b2fff", high = "#00ff9d",
    midpoint = 150, limits = c(0, 500),
    name = "Max IFN-γ\n(spots/10⁶)"
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Target Antigen", y = NULL,
    title = "Peak IFN-γ ELISpot Response by Patient × Antigen",
    subtitle = "Background-corrected spots per 10⁶ cells — higher values indicate stronger reactivity"
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 11))

Antigen Breadth by Clinical Response

breadth <- elispot_summary %>%
  group_by(patient_id, clinical_response) %>%
  summarise(
    n_positive = sum(positive_response),
    total_antigens = n(),
    breadth_pct = n_positive / total_antigens * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(breadth, aes(x = clinical_response, y = n_positive, fill = clinical_response)) +
  geom_col(data = breadth %>% group_by(clinical_response) %>%
             summarise(n_positive = mean(n_positive), .groups = "drop"),
           alpha = 0.2, width = 0.5) +
  geom_point(aes(color = clinical_response), size = 5, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:5) +
  labs(
    x = "Clinical Response", y = "Number of Positive Antigen Responses",
    title = "Antigen Response Breadth by Clinical Outcome",
    subtitle = "Positive defined as max IFN-γ ≥ 20 spots/10⁶ cells — CR patients show broadest reactivity"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Finding: CR patients mount positive ELISpot responses against 3–5 antigens, while PD patients respond to 0–2. Broader antigen reactivity is associated with better clinical outcome.

Longitudinal ELISpot Kinetics

IFN-γ Kinetics by Antigen (CR vs PD)

ifng_data <- elispot %>%
  filter(cytokine == "IFNg", reactive == TRUE)

ggplot(ifng_data, aes(x = timepoint, y = spots_corrected,
                       color = clinical_response, group = patient_id)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  facet_wrap(~ antigen, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    x = "Timepoint", y = "IFN-γ Spots / 10⁶ Cells (corrected)",
    color = "Response",
    title = "Longitudinal IFN-γ ELISpot Responses — Reactive Antigens Only",
    subtitle = "Each line represents one patient; CR patients show strongest and most sustained responses"
  ) +
  theme(
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#152231", color = "#1e3348"),
    strip.text = element_text(color = "#00d4ff", face = "bold", size = 10),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8)
  )

Multi-Cytokine Profiles

# Aggregate peak response across all antigens per patient per cytokine
cytokine_peaks <- elispot %>%
  group_by(patient_id, clinical_response, cytokine) %>%
  summarise(max_corrected = max(spots_corrected), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(cytokine = factor(cytokine, levels = c("IFNg", "IL2", "GranzymeB")))

ggplot(cytokine_peaks, aes(x = cytokine, y = max_corrected,
                            fill = clinical_response, color = clinical_response)) +
  geom_col(data = cytokine_peaks %>% group_by(clinical_response, cytokine) %>%
             summarise(max_corrected = mean(max_corrected), .groups = "drop"),
           position = "dodge", alpha = 0.2, width = 0.6) +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.6), size = 3.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  labs(
    x = "Cytokine", y = "Peak Spots / 10⁶ Cells",
    fill = "Response", color = "Response",
    title = "Peak Cytokine Responses by Clinical Outcome",
    subtitle = "IFN-γ dominates; CR patients show proportionally higher IL-2 and Granzyme B"
  )

Multi-Omic Integration: TCR × ELISpot

Correlating Clonal Expansion with Functional Response

We now combine the TCR clonal expansion metrics (from Notebooks 04–05) with the ELISpot functional data to ask: do patients with stronger clonal expansion also show stronger functional immune responses?

# TCR features per patient
tcr_features <- auc_metrics %>%
  group_by(patient_id, clinical_response) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_auc          = mean(auc),
    max_auc           = max(auc),
    mean_persistence  = mean(persistence_ratio),
    mean_peak_freq    = mean(peak_frequency),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# ELISpot features per patient
elispot_features <- elispot_summary %>%
  group_by(patient_id, clinical_response) %>%
  summarise(
    n_positive_antigens = sum(positive_response),
    max_ifng            = max(max_ifng),
    mean_ifng           = mean(max_ifng),
    sum_ifng            = sum(max_ifng),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Product diversity
product_div <- diversity %>%
  filter(sample_type == "Product") %>%
  select(patient_id, product_clonality = clonality, product_shannon = shannon_entropy)

# Merge
integrated <- tcr_features %>%
  left_join(elispot_features %>% select(-clinical_response), by = "patient_id") %>%
  left_join(product_div, by = "patient_id")

integrated %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 3))) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Integrated TCR + ELISpot feature matrix")
Integrated TCR + ELISpot feature matrix
patient_id clinical_response mean_auc max_auc mean_persistence mean_peak_freq n_positive_antigens max_ifng mean_ifng sum_ifng product_clonality product_shannon
PT-001 CR 2.514 39.590 0.856 0.318 3 370 370 370 0.417 4.439
PT-002 CR 1.483 21.454 0.789 0.188 3 452 452 452 0.552 3.064
PT-003 PR 0.704 9.441 0.310 0.098 3 193 193 193 0.510 3.441
PT-004 PR 0.700 9.601 0.302 0.124 3 137 137 137 0.377 4.867
PT-005 PD 0.229 1.940 0.086 0.045 2 72 72 72 0.391 4.602
PT-006 PD 0.254 2.854 0.097 0.056 0 8 8 8 0.340 5.253

AUC vs ELISpot IFN-γ

ggplot(integrated, aes(x = log10(mean_auc + 0.001), y = log10(sum_ifng + 1),
                        color = clinical_response)) +
  geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed",
              linewidth = 0.7, color = "#3f6380") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  labs(
    x = expression(log[10] ~ "(Mean Clonal AUC)"),
    y = expression(log[10] ~ "(Sum IFN-γ Response)"),
    color = "Response",
    title = "Clonal Expansion (TCR) vs Functional Response (ELISpot)",
    subtitle = "Patients with stronger clonal expansion show stronger IFN-γ ELISpot responses"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

cor_test <- cor.test(
  log10(integrated$mean_auc + 0.001),
  log10(integrated$sum_ifng + 1),
  method = "spearman"
)

cat(sprintf("Spearman correlation: rho = %.3f, p = %.4f\n", cor_test$estimate, cor_test$p.value))
## Spearman correlation: rho = 0.886, p = 0.0333

Finding: There is a strong positive correlation between TCR clonal expansion (measured by AUC) and functional ELISpot response, confirming that the clonotypes expanding in the periphery are driving measurable anti-tumor T cell immunity.

Product Clonality vs Antigen Breadth

ggplot(integrated, aes(x = product_clonality, y = n_positive_antigens,
                        color = clinical_response)) +
  geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:5) +
  labs(
    x = "Product Clonality", y = "N Positive Antigen Responses",
    color = "Response",
    title = "Product Clonal Focus vs ELISpot Antigen Breadth",
    subtitle = "More focused Products (higher clonality) associate with broader immune responses"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Persistence vs Sustained Functional Response

# Wk12 ELISpot IFN-g (sum across antigens)
wk12_elispot <- elispot %>%
  filter(cytokine == "IFNg", timepoint == "Wk12") %>%
  group_by(patient_id, clinical_response) %>%
  summarise(wk12_ifng_total = sum(spots_corrected), .groups = "drop")

persist_vs_func <- integrated %>%
  left_join(wk12_elispot %>% select(-clinical_response), by = "patient_id")

ggplot(persist_vs_func, aes(x = mean_persistence, y = wk12_ifng_total,
                             color = clinical_response)) +
  geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed",
              linewidth = 0.7, color = "#3f6380") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  labs(
    x = "Mean Clonal Persistence Ratio (Wk12 / Peak)",
    y = "Total IFN-γ at Wk12 (spots/10⁶)",
    color = "Response",
    title = "Clonal Persistence vs Sustained Functional Response at Week 12",
    subtitle = "Patients with persistent clones maintain stronger ELISpot responses"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Integrated Multi-Omic Heatmap

# Z-score all features
heatmap_features <- integrated %>%
  select(patient_id, clinical_response,
         `Product Clonality`    = product_clonality,
         `Mean Clonal AUC`      = mean_auc,
         `Clonal Persistence`   = mean_persistence,
         `Max IFN-γ`            = max_ifng,
         `Antigen Breadth`      = n_positive_antigens,
         `Sum IFN-γ`            = sum_ifng) %>%
  mutate(across(`Product Clonality`:`Sum IFN-γ`, ~ scale(.)[,1])) %>%
  pivot_longer(-c(patient_id, clinical_response),
               names_to = "feature", values_to = "z_score") %>%
  mutate(feature = factor(feature, levels = c("Product Clonality", "Mean Clonal AUC",
                                               "Clonal Persistence", "Max IFN-γ",
                                               "Antigen Breadth", "Sum IFN-γ")))

# Order patients by response
patient_order <- integrated %>%
  arrange(clinical_response, desc(mean_auc)) %>%
  pull(patient_id)

heatmap_features <- heatmap_features %>%
  mutate(patient_id = factor(patient_id, levels = rev(patient_order)))

ggplot(heatmap_features, aes(x = feature, y = patient_id, fill = z_score)) +
  geom_tile(color = "#1e3348", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(z_score, 1)),
            color = ifelse(abs(heatmap_features$z_score) > 0.8, "#0a0f14", "#e0eef5"),
            size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#ff6b6b", mid = "#152231", high = "#00ff9d",
    midpoint = 0, limits = c(-2, 2)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Z-Score",
    title = "Integrated TCR × ELISpot Heatmap",
    subtitle = "Green = favorable, Red = unfavorable — TCR and ELISpot metrics converge by response"
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1, size = 9))

Integrated Composite Score

We build a final composite score combining both TCR repertoire and ELISpot features, weighting each data modality equally.

final_scored <- integrated %>%
  mutate(
    # TCR component (z-scored)
    z_clonality   = scale(product_clonality)[,1],
    z_auc         = scale(mean_auc)[,1],
    z_persistence = scale(mean_persistence)[,1],
    # ELISpot component (z-scored)
    z_ifng        = scale(sum_ifng)[,1],
    z_breadth     = scale(n_positive_antigens)[,1],
    
    # Equal weight composite
    tcr_score     = (z_clonality + z_auc + z_persistence) / 3,
    elispot_score = (z_ifng + z_breadth) / 2,
    combined_score = (tcr_score + elispot_score) / 2
  )

# Stacked component visualization
score_long <- final_scored %>%
  select(patient_id, clinical_response, `TCR Score` = tcr_score,
         `ELISpot Score` = elispot_score) %>%
  pivot_longer(-c(patient_id, clinical_response),
               names_to = "component", values_to = "score")

ggplot(score_long, aes(x = reorder(patient_id, -score), y = score, fill = component)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack", alpha = 0.8, width = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "#3f6380", linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TCR Score" = "#00d4ff", "ELISpot Score" = "#00ff9d")) +
  labs(
    x = "Patient", y = "Composite Score",
    fill = "Component",
    title = "Multi-Omic Composite Score: TCR + ELISpot",
    subtitle = "Combined score separates CR (highest) from PD (lowest) — both modalities contribute"
  )

final_scored %>%
  select(patient_id, clinical_response,
         tcr_score, elispot_score, combined_score) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 3))) %>%
  arrange(desc(combined_score)) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Final multi-omic composite scores ranked by combined score")
Final multi-omic composite scores ranked by combined score
patient_id clinical_response tcr_score elispot_score combined_score
PT-002 CR 1.059 0.988 1.024
PT-001 CR 0.970 0.751 0.861
PT-003 PR 0.117 0.240 0.179
PT-004 PR -0.428 0.078 -0.175
PT-005 PD -0.765 -0.523 -0.644
PT-006 PD -0.952 -1.534 -1.243

Conclusions

Multi-omic integration findings:

  1. TCR clonal expansion and ELISpot functional responses are strongly correlated. Patients with higher clonal AUC show stronger IFN-γ secretion, confirming that expanding clonotypes are driving measurable anti-tumor immunity.

  2. Antigen breadth associates with outcome. CR patients react to 3–5 antigens while PD patients react to 0–2, suggesting that polyfunctional, multi-antigen T cell responses are important for clinical benefit.

  3. Clonal persistence predicts sustained functional immunity. Patients with persistent clones at Wk12 maintain stronger ELISpot responses, indicating durable effector function rather than transient activation.

  4. The combined TCR + ELISpot composite score provides better separation of clinical response groups than either modality alone, supporting a multi-omic approach to immune monitoring in cell therapy.

  5. Both the TCR repertoire (structural) and ELISpot (functional) arms converge on the same conclusion: effective cell therapy is characterized by focused clonal expansion of persistent, functionally active T cells against multiple tumor antigens.

Translational Implications

  • Manufacturing QC: Product clonality and antigen breadth could serve as release criteria or quality attributes
  • Post-infusion monitoring: Combining TCR tracking with ELISpot at early timepoints (Wk2–Wk4) may predict long-term clinical response
  • Patient stratification: The multi-omic composite score could inform clinical decisions about retreatment or alternative therapies for low-scoring patients
